content:
# Numbers Chapter 11 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 160-162 | Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0120-0121 (LXX)
**Source Images:** `NUM_011_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_011_page_002.jpg`, `NUM_011_page_003.jpg`; `NUM_011_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_011_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The People Complain; Fire at Taberah (vv. 1-3)

**1** And the people were as those who complain of evil in the ears of YHWH. And YHWH heard, and his anger was kindled, and the fire of YHWH burned among them and consumed at the edge of the camp.

**2** And the people cried out to Moses, and Moses prayed to YHWH, and the fire sank down.

**3** And he called the name of that place Taberah, because the fire of YHWH burned among them.

---

### The Mixed Multitude and the Craving for Meat (vv. 4-9)

**4** And the mixed multitude that was in their midst craved a craving, and the Sons of Israel also wept again and said, "Who will feed us meat?

**5** We remember the fish that we ate in Egypt for nothing, the cucumbers, and the melons, and the leeks, and the onions, and the garlic.

**6** But now our soul is dried up; there is nothing at all -- only this manna before our eyes."

**7** And the manna was like coriander seed, and its appearance like the appearance of bdellium.

**8** The people went about and gathered it and ground it with millstones or crushed it in the mortar and boiled it in the pot and made cakes of it; and its taste was like the taste of cakes baked in oil.

**9** And when the dew fell upon the camp at night, the manna fell upon it.

---

### Moses' Burden and Complaint (vv. 10-15)

**10** And Moses heard the people weeping according to their families, each man at the entrance of his tent. And the anger of YHWH was kindled greatly, and in the eyes of Moses it was evil.

**11** And Moses said to YHWH, "Why have you done evil to your servant? And why have I not found favor in your eyes, that you lay the burden of all this people upon me?

**12** Did I conceive all this people? Did I give them birth, that you should say to me, 'Carry them in your bosom, as the nursing father carries the suckling child,' to the land that you swore to their fathers?

**13** From where shall I get meat to give to all this people? For they weep before me, saying, 'Give us meat that we may eat.'

**14** I am not able to bear all this people alone, for it is too heavy for me.

**15** And if thus you are doing to me, please kill me altogether, if I have found favor in your eyes, and let me not see my evil."

---

### YHWH's Response: Seventy Elders (vv. 16-17)

**16** And YHWH said to Moses, "Gather for me seventy men from the elders of Israel, whom you know to be the elders of the people and their officers, and bring them to the tent of meeting, and let them station themselves there with you.

**17** And I will come down and speak with you there, and I will take of the Spirit that is upon you and put it upon them, and they shall bear the burden of the people with you, and you shall not bear it alone."

---

### The Promise of Meat (vv. 18-23)

**18** And to the people you shall say, 'Consecrate yourselves for tomorrow, and you shall eat meat, for you have wept in the ears of YHWH, saying, "Who will feed us meat? For it was good for us in Egypt." And YHWH will give you meat, and you shall eat.

**19** You shall not eat one day, nor two days, nor five days, nor ten days, nor twenty days,

**20** but a whole month of days, until it comes out of your nostrils and becomes loathsome to you, because you have rejected YHWH who is in your midst and have wept before him, saying, "Why is it that we came out of Egypt?"'"

**21** And Moses said, "Six hundred thousand footmen are the people in whose midst I am, and you have said, 'I will give them meat, and they shall eat a whole month of days.'

**22** Shall flocks and herds be slaughtered for them, that it should suffice for them? Or shall all the fish of the sea be gathered for them, that it should suffice for them?"

**23** And YHWH said to Moses, "Is the hand of YHWH too short? Now you shall see whether my word will come to pass for you or not."

---

### The Spirit upon the Seventy Elders (vv. 24-25)

**24** And Moses went out and spoke to the people the words of YHWH. And he gathered seventy men from the elders of the people and stationed them around the tent.

**25** And YHWH came down in the cloud and spoke to him, and he took of the Spirit that was upon him and put it upon the seventy men, the elders. And it came to pass, when the Spirit rested upon them, that they prophesied, but they did not continue.

---

### Eldad and Medad Prophesy in the Camp (vv. 26-30)

**26** And two men had remained in the camp; the name of the one was Eldad, and the name of the second was Medad. And the Spirit rested upon them -- and they were among those written down, but they had not gone out to the tent -- and they prophesied in the camp.

**27** And a young man ran and told Moses, and said, "Eldad and Medad are prophesying in the camp."

**28** And Joshua the son of Nun, the attendant of Moses from his chosen ones, answered and said, "My lord Moses, restrain them!"

**29** And Moses said to him, "Are you jealous for my sake? Would that all the people of YHWH were prophets, that YHWH would put his Spirit upon them!"

**30** And Moses gathered himself into the camp, he and the elders of Israel.

---

### The Quail and the Plague (vv. 31-35)

**31** And a wind went out from YHWH, and it drove quail from the sea and let them fall beside the camp, about a day's journey on this side and about a day's journey on the other side, around the camp, and about two cubits above the face of the ground.

**32** And the people rose up all that day and all the night and all the next day, and gathered the quail. He who gathered least gathered ten homers. And they spread them out for themselves all around the camp.

**33** The meat was still between their teeth, before it was chewed, when the anger of YHWH was kindled against the people, and YHWH struck the people with a very great plague.

**34** And he called the name of that place Kibroth-hattaavah, because there they buried the people who had the craving.

**35** From Kibroth-hattaavah the people journeyed to Hazeroth, and they were at Hazeroth.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **Verse 1 -- "as those who complain of evil" (כְּמִתְאֹנְנִים רַע):** The Hebrew participle mit'onenim is from the Hithpael of אנן, a rare root occurring only here in the Hebrew Bible. Its precise meaning is debated: "murmurers," "complainers," or those who feign misfortune. The word ra' (evil) functions adverbially, indicating the complaint was of an evil or displeasing character. The LXX renders γογγυζων ο λαος πονηρα εναντι κυριου (the people were murmuring wicked things before the Lord), interpreting the participle as standard murmuring. The formal rendering preserves the unusual syntax of the MT, where the people are described "as" (ke-) complainers rather than directly identified as such -- a subtle narrative distancing.

2. **Verse 1 -- "the fire of YHWH" (אֵשׁ יְהוָה):** This fire is divine judgment -- not lightning or a natural fire, but fire identified as belonging to YHWH, the same designation used for the fire that consumed the offering at Leviticus 9:24. The LXX renders πυρ παρα κυριου (fire from the Lord), adding a preposition that specifies origin. The MT's construct chain ("fire of YHWH") is more ambiguous and more terrible: the fire is YHWH's own. The burning "at the edge of the camp" (בִּקְצֵה הַמַּחֲנֶה) may indicate the periphery where the mixed multitude (v. 4) was encamped.

3. **Verse 2 -- "the fire sank down" (וַתִּשְׁקַע הָאֵשׁ):** The verb שׁקע means to sink, subside, or be swallowed up. The LXX renders κατεσβη το πυρ (the fire was extinguished). The Hebrew image is more vivid: the fire sinks into the ground rather than simply going out, as if swallowed back by the earth. This verb occurs elsewhere only in Amos 8:8 and 9:5 (of the Nile rising and sinking).

4. **Verse 3 -- "Taberah" (תַּבְעֵרָה):** From the root בער (to burn). The name is an etiological marker: the place was called "Burning" because YHWH's fire burned (ba'arah) there. The LXX renders Εμπυρισμος (Conflagration), translating rather than transliterating. The Leningrad Codex vocalizes the name with pathach under the tav and segol under the ayin. Taberah is not listed in the itinerary of Numbers 33, suggesting it may be an alternate name for the campsite rather than a separate location.

5. **Verse 4 -- "the mixed multitude" (הָאסַפְסֻף):** The Hebrew ha'safsuf is a hapax legomenon -- occurring only here. It is an intensive reduplicated form related to אסף (to gather), denoting a gathered rabble or mixed collection of people. This is distinct from the erev rav (mixed multitude) of Exodus 12:38, though referring to the same or a similar group of non-Israelites who accompanied the exodus. The LXX renders ο επιμικτος (the mixed ones), connecting it to the tradition in Exodus. The formal rendering "mixed multitude" conveys the sense of a heterogeneous group while preserving the distinction from erev rav in the notes.

6. **Verse 4 -- "craved a craving" (הִתְאַוּוּ תַּאֲוָה):** A cognate accusative construction (figura etymologica) intensifying the verb: they desired with desire. The LXX renders επεθυμησαν επιθυμιαν (desired a desire), replicating the Hebrew idiom in Greek. This intense craving becomes the thematic thread of the chapter, culminating in the name Kibroth-hattaavah (Graves of Craving, v. 34).

7. **Verse 5 -- The catalogue of Egyptian foods:** Five specific vegetables are named: qishshu'im (cucumbers), avattichim (melons/watermelons), chatsir (leeks), betsolim (onions), and shumim (garlic). All five are well-attested as staples of ancient Egyptian agriculture. The LXX renders σικυους, πεπονας, πρασα, κρομμυα, σκορδα -- all standard Greek equivalents. The word chinnam (for nothing/freely) suggests the abundance and low cost of Egyptian provisions, contrasting with the monotony of wilderness manna. The nostalgic idealization of Egyptian bondage is a recurring motif in the wilderness narratives (Exodus 16:3; Numbers 14:2-4; 21:5).

8. **Verse 6 -- "our soul is dried up" (נַפְשֵׁנוּ יְבֵשָׁה):** The nephesh (appetite/life-force) is described as yaveshah (dried out, withered). This is visceral language of depletion and despair. The LXX renders η ψυχη ημων κατεξηραμμενη (our soul is utterly dried up), intensifying with a compound form. The physical dryness mirrors their spiritual condition.

9. **Verse 7 -- "bdellium" (הַבְּדֹלַח):** The Hebrew bedolach appears only here and in Genesis 2:12, where it is associated with the land of Havilah alongside gold and onyx. Its identification remains uncertain: classical interpreters suggested a precious resin (bdellium), while others propose a pearl or crystal. The LXX renders ειδος κρυσταλλου (appearance of crystal). The comparison describes the manna's translucent, pale appearance. The Leningrad Codex reading is clear.

10. **Verse 8 -- "millstones...mortar...pot...cakes" (רֵחַיִם...מְדֹכָה...פָּרוּר...עֻגוֹת):** Four stages of manna preparation are described: grinding in hand-mills, crushing in a mortar, boiling in a cooking pot, and forming into cakes. The versatility of the manna's preparation contrasts with the people's complaint of monotony. The LXX renders μυλος...ιγδιον...χυτρα...εγκρυφιας. The term leshad hashamen (taste of cakes baked in oil, literally "the moistness of oil") suggests a rich, moist texture. The parenthetical description of manna (vv. 7-9) interrupts the complaint narrative to present the reader with the objective reality that the people's dissatisfaction distorts.

11. **Verse 10 -- "weeping according to their families" (בֹּכֶה לְמִשְׁפְּחֹתָיו):** The weeping is organized by clan groups -- not spontaneous individual grief but communal, orchestrated lamentation. The phrase le-mishpechotav (according to his families) suggests each family unit gathered at its tent entrance to weep publicly. The LXX renders κατα δημους αυτων (according to their peoples). The Talmud (Yoma 75a) interprets "according to their families" as weeping "about family matters" -- that is, about the sexual prohibitions newly imposed at Sinai -- though the contextual meaning is spatial and social organization.

12. **Verse 12 -- "Did I conceive all this people?" (הֶאָנֹכִי הָרִיתִי אֵת כָּל־הָעָם הַזֶּה):** Moses employs maternal imagery -- conception (harah) and birth (yalad) -- to protest his assigned role. The rhetorical questions expect negative answers: Moses is not the parent of this people; YHWH is. The nursing-father image (ha'omen, from אמן) that follows is masculine, describing a male guardian carrying an infant. The LXX renders εγω εν γαστρι ελαβον παντα τον λαον τουτον (Did I take all this people in the womb?). The passage is remarkable for its application of both maternal and paternal parenting metaphors to the divine-human relationship.

13. **Verse 15 -- "let me not see my evil" (וְאַל־אֶרְאֶה בְּרָעָתִי):** The word ra'ati could mean "my wretchedness" (the evil done to me) or "my evil" (my own misery/failure). Some manuscripts and versions read be-ra'atam ("their evil"), softening Moses' despair. The LXX reads την κακωσιν μου (my affliction), supporting the first reading. The Leningrad Codex clearly reads the first-person singular suffix. Moses' death wish (kill me altogether, הָרְגֵנִי נָא הָרֹג) is paralleled by Elijah's request in 1 Kings 19:4. The infinitive absolute harog preceding the imperative horgeni intensifies the plea: "utterly kill me."

14. **Verse 16 -- "seventy men from the elders of Israel":** The number seventy recalls the seventy elders who ascended Sinai with Moses (Exodus 24:1, 9) and the seventy descendants of Jacob who entered Egypt (Genesis 46:27; Exodus 1:5). Whether these are the same individuals as the Sinai elders or a new selection is not specified. The LXX renders εβδομηκοντα ανδρας απο των πρεσβυτερων Ισραηλ. The institution here described may be the antecedent of the later Sanhedrin of seventy-one members (Sanhedrin 1:6).

15. **Verse 17 -- "I will take of the Spirit that is upon you" (וְאָצַלְתִּי מִן־הָרוּחַ אֲשֶׁר עָלֶיךָ):** The verb אצל (to set aside, withdraw, take from) implies a distribution of the same Spirit already resting on Moses, not a new endowment. The preposition min (from/of) indicates a partitive action. The LXX renders αφελω απο του πνευματος του επι σοι (I will take away from the spirit that is upon you). The rabbis debated whether this diminished Moses' spirit: Rabbi Yehuda likened it to a candle lighting other candles without diminishing (Sanhedrin 17a; Sifrei Beha'alotekha 92). The text itself is silent on whether Moses' endowment decreased.

16. **Verse 20 -- "until it comes out of your nostrils" (עַד אֲשֶׁר־יֵצֵא מֵאַפְּכֶם):** A graphic, almost grotesque image of forced satiation to the point of revulsion. The noun af means both "nose" and "anger" -- a deliberate ambiguity, since YHWH's anger (af) is the cause of the meat that will come out of their noses (af). The LXX renders εως αν εξελθη εκ των μυκτηρων υμων (until it comes out of your nostrils). The word za'ra (loathsome) connotes nausea and rejection. The punishment matches the sin: those who craved will be given their craving until it becomes their torment.

17. **Verse 22 -- Moses' incredulity:** Moses calculates the impossibility: six hundred thousand foot soldiers (not counting women, children, and the mixed multitude). His two rhetorical questions -- slaughtering all flocks and herds, gathering all fish of the sea -- express the scope of the logistical impossibility. The LXX follows closely. This moment of doubt from Moses parallels Abraham's laughter (Genesis 17:17) and Sarah's laughter (Genesis 18:12) at similarly impossible divine promises.

18. **Verse 23 -- "Is the hand of YHWH too short?" (הֲיַד יְהוָה תִּקְצָר):** The divine response is a question that rebukes Moses' calculation. The "hand" of YHWH is the standard biblical metaphor for divine power; "too short" (qatsar, from קצר, to be short/insufficient) suggests inadequacy. The question expects the answer "No." The LXX renders μη χειρ κυριου ουκ εξαρκεσει (Shall the hand of the Lord not be sufficient?). The same idiom appears in Isaiah 50:2 and 59:1. YHWH's power is not measured by human arithmetic.

19. **Verse 25 -- "they prophesied, but they did not continue" (וַיִּתְנַבְּאוּ וְלֹא יָסָפוּ):** The Hithpael of נבא indicates ecstatic or inspired speech. The phrase ve-lo yasafu (and they did not add/continue) is debated: (a) they prophesied on this occasion only and never again, or (b) they did not cease (reading lo' as an emphatic particle rather than a negative, as in Deuteronomy 5:22). The LXX renders και ουκετι προσεθεντο (and they did not continue again), supporting reading (a). The formal rendering follows the majority interpretation: the prophetic experience was a one-time validating sign, not a permanent charism.

20. **Verse 26 -- "among those written down" (בַּכְּתֻבִים):** Eldad and Medad were among those registered or inscribed on the list of seventy elders but did not go out to the tent of meeting. The LXX renders ουτοι ησαν των καταγεγραμμενων (these were among those who had been registered). Why they remained in the camp is not stated; the Talmud (Sanhedrin 17a) proposes that seventy-two were originally chosen (six from each tribe), and two voluntarily withdrew out of humility, considering themselves unworthy. Despite their absence from the tent, the Spirit rested upon them, demonstrating that the divine endowment is not constrained by spatial proximity to the sanctuary.

21. **Verse 28 -- "from his chosen ones" (מִבְּחֻרָיו):** The Hebrew mibechurav is difficult. It could mean "from his youth" (i.e., Joshua had been Moses' attendant since his youth), reading bechurim as "youth/young men," or "from his chosen ones" (i.e., Joshua was one of Moses' select attendants). The LXX renders ο εκλεκτος Μωυση (the chosen one of Moses), reading it as a singular description of Joshua's status. The Leningrad Codex vocalization supports "from his chosen ones." The formal rendering follows the MT vocalization.

22. **Verse 28 -- "restrain them" (כְּלָאֵם):** The verb כלא means to shut up, restrain, or imprison. Joshua views the unsanctioned prophesying in the camp as a threat to Moses' unique authority. The LXX renders κωλυσον αυτους (prevent them). Joshua's protective instinct parallels the disciples' response in Mark 9:38 and Luke 9:49 to unauthorized exorcism. Moses' reply (v. 29) redefines prophetic authority: it is not a threat to his leadership but a fulfillment of the divine purpose.

23. **Verse 29 -- "Would that all the people of YHWH were prophets" (וּמִי יִתֵּן כָּל־עַם יְהוָה נְבִיאִים):** The optative formula mi yitten (who will give = would that) expresses Moses' desire for the universal distribution of the Spirit. This is one of the most theologically significant statements in the Pentateuch regarding the Spirit of YHWH. The LXX renders τις δωη παντα τον λαον κυριου προφητας (who would give that all the people of the Lord were prophets). Joel 2:28-29 [Hebrew 3:1-2] echoes this wish as a future promise, and the early church understood Pentecost (Acts 2:16-18) as its fulfillment.

24. **Verse 31 -- "about two cubits above the face of the ground" (כְּאַמָּתַיִם עַל־פְּנֵי הָאָרֶץ):** The quail flew at approximately two cubits (roughly three feet or one meter) above the ground surface, making them easily caught by hand or with simple tools. The LXX renders ωσει δυο πηχεις απο της γης (about two cubits from the earth). Some interpreters read this as the depth to which the quail piled up on the ground rather than their flight height; the Hebrew al-penei (upon the face of) favors flight altitude. The wind from YHWH (ruach me'et YHWH) drove them from the sea -- presumably the Gulf of Aqaba or the Mediterranean -- matching the known migratory routes of European quail (Coturnix coturnix), which cross the Sinai Peninsula in spring.

25. **Verse 32 -- "ten homers" (עֲשָׂרָה חֳמָרִים):** A homer (chomer) is the largest dry measure in the Hebrew system, equivalent to approximately two hundred twenty liters or six bushels. Ten homers would therefore be approximately sixty bushels or twenty-two hundred liters -- an enormous quantity. The LXX renders δεκα κορους (ten kors), using the Greek equivalent measure. The specification that this was the minimum gathered ("he who gathered least") emphasizes the overwhelming abundance of the divine provision, which will become the instrument of judgment.

26. **Verse 33 -- "before it was chewed" (טֶרֶם יִכָּרֵת):** Literally "before it was cut off" -- that is, before the supply was cut off or consumed. The verb כרת (to cut) has been variously interpreted: (a) before the meat was cut off from between their teeth (i.e., while still chewing), (b) before the supply of meat was exhausted or cut off, or (c) before the meat was digested. The LXX renders πριν η εκλειπειν (before it gave out/failed), supporting reading (b). The formal rendering "before it was chewed" follows the traditional interpretation of the immediate dental imagery. The theological point is clear in all readings: the judgment was swift, arriving while the craving was still being indulged.

27. **Verse 34 -- "Kibroth-hattaavah" (קִבְרוֹת הַתַּאֲוָה):** Literally "Graves of the Craving." The compound name (qiverot + ha-ta'avah) forms an etiological notice: the place name memorializes both the sin (craving) and the punishment (burial). The LXX renders Μνηματα της Επιθυμιας (Memorials/Graves of Desire), translating rather than transliterating. This site is listed in the wilderness itinerary at Numbers 33:16-17 between the wilderness of Sinai and Hazeroth, and is referenced again at Deuteronomy 9:22 as an example of Israel's rebellions.

28. **Verse 35 -- "Hazeroth" (חֲצֵרוֹת):** From the root חצר (enclosure, courtyard), meaning "enclosures" or "settlements." This becomes the setting for the next narrative unit (Numbers 12), in which Miriam and Aaron challenge Moses' authority. The LXX renders Ασηρωθ. The itinerary note functions as a geographic suture linking the Kibroth-hattaavah episode to what follows.

### Structural Observations

- Numbers 11 is structured as a diptych of crisis and divine response. The first panel (vv. 1-3) presents a brief episode of complaint, fire, intercession, and cessation -- a compressed prototype of the larger pattern. The second panel (vv. 4-35) expands this pattern into a complex narrative involving multiple characters and two distinct divine actions: the appointment of the seventy elders (resolving Moses' crisis) and the provision of quail followed by plague (responding to the people's craving). The chapter thus presents two problems -- the burden on Moses and the appetite of the people -- and two solutions, one gracious and one punitive.

- The parenthetical manna description (vv. 7-9) is a narrative aside addressed to the reader, interrupting the people's complaint to provide an objective counter-testimony. The manna was versatile, nutritious, and divinely provided -- the very opposite of "nothing at all" (v. 6). This editorial insertion functions as an implicit rebuke of the people's ingratitude.

- Moses' complaint (vv. 11-15) is one of the most psychologically raw passages in the Torah. He uses maternal imagery (conception, birth, nursing) to protest that YHWH, not he, is the parent of this people. The climax is a death wish (v. 15) that mirrors the despair of Elijah (1 Kings 19:4) and Jonah (Jonah 4:3). YHWH's response (vv. 16-17) addresses not the emotional distress but its structural cause: the burden is redistributed, not removed.

- The Eldad-and-Medad episode (vv. 26-29) is a theological digression with far-reaching implications. Joshua's instinct to suppress unauthorized prophesying reflects an institutional view of spiritual authority; Moses' response ("Would that all the people of YHWH were prophets") envisions a democratization of the Spirit that transcends institutional mediation. This tension between institutional and charismatic authority recurs throughout biblical history (cf. 1 Samuel 10:10-12; 19:20-24; Joel 2:28-29; Acts 2:17-18).

- The quail narrative (vv. 31-34) inverts the manna provision of Exodus 16. Where the manna was given graciously and gathered in measured portions, the quail are given in wrath and gathered in excess. The minimum quantity (ten homers) is grotesque abundance. The plague strikes while the meat is still between their teeth -- the craving is punished at the very moment of its satisfaction. The place name Kibroth-hattaavah (Graves of Craving) transforms geography into moral warning: desire itself becomes a grave.

- The chapter's movement from Taberah (Burning, v. 3) to Kibroth-hattaavah (Graves of Craving, v. 34) to Hazeroth (Enclosures, v. 35) traces a geographic and narrative descent. Each place name encodes a judgment: fire, death, and confinement. The wilderness is becoming a landscape of divine displeasure, foreshadowing the greater judgment at Kadesh-barnea (Numbers 13-14) that will consign an entire generation to death in this same wilderness.
